summary: In this paper, Anil Gomes explores Iris Murdoch's philosophy on the intersection of art, ethics, and attention. Murdoch argues that aesthetic and ethical values are deeply intertwined, relying on 'thick' concepts that blend description and evaluation. She posits that communal attention to art can serve as a vehicle for 'unselfing' and understanding the moral frameworks of others.

principles.statement: Goodness and beauty are part of the same structure.
principles.rationale: Aesthetic situations are actual cases of morals, requiring the same virtues (bravery, truthfulness, patience, humility) as ethical behavior.
principles.source_quote: Goodness and beauty are not to be contrasted but are largely part of the same structure

principles.statement: Value concepts are inextricably tied to the world.
principles.rationale: Separating fact from value wrongly reduces value to mere emotive language or attitudes, ignoring that the world itself dictates appropriate evaluative attitudes.

principles.statement: Understanding others requires shared contexts and common objects of attention.
principles.rationale: Human beings are morally obscure to one another unless they can jointly attend to something, like a work of art, to bridge their conceptual schemes.
principles.source_quote: Human beings are obscure to each other, in certain respects which are particularly relevant to morality, unless they are mutual objects of attention or have common objects of attention…

frameworks.name: Joint Attention for Conceptual Understanding
frameworks.when_to_apply: When trying to understand the aesthetic or ethical framework of another person or culture.
steps: Group around a common object of attention (e.g., a work of art).
Listen to normative-descriptive talk from an informed other in the presence of the object.
Share their context to gradually grasp the beliefs, concerns, and values that structure their concepts.
frameworks.source_quote: Uses of words by persons grouped round a common object is a central and vital human activity. The art critic can help us if we are in the presence of the same object and if we know something about his scheme of concepts.

mental_models.name: Unselfing
mental_models.description: A process of moral and aesthetic improvement where one draws away from the ego and personal prejudices to direct a 'just and loving gaze' upon reality.
mental_models.example: Attending closely to a great painting or listening quietly to music to develop a judicious, respectful sensibility that transfers to ethical deliberation.

mental_models.name: Monism of Value
mental_models.description: The belief that all forms of value—whether aesthetic, ethical, or intellectual—stem from a single, unitary structure rather than existing in isolated domains.
mental_models.example: Recognizing that the virtues required to be a good artist (bravery, truthfulness, humility) are fundamentally the same as those required to be a good person.

heuristics: Virtue is au fond the same in the artist as in the good man.
Art and morals are, with certain provisos… one.

signature_quotes.text: Human beings are obscure to each other, in certain respects which are particularly relevant to morality, unless they are mutual objects of attention or have common objects of attention…
signature_quotes.context: On the necessity of shared attention for moral understanding.

signature_quotes.text: Goodness and beauty are not to be contrasted but are largely part of the same structure.
signature_quotes.context: On the relationship between aesthetics and ethics.

signature_quotes.text: aesthetic situations are not so much analogies of morals as cases of morals.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how art appreciation exercises moral virtues.

signature_quotes.text: The good artist, in relation to his art, is brave, truthful, patient, humble.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the virtues inherent in artistic creation.

signature_quotes.text: a just and loving gaze directed upon an individual reality
signature_quotes.context: Describing the nature of true attention.

signature_quotes.text: It is the traditional inspiration of the philosopher, but also his traditional vice, to believe that all is one
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on her own tendency toward philosophical monism.

anti_patterns.description: Divorcing value claims from factual propositions (the fact/value distinction).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It relegates value to a 'shadowy existence in terms of emotive language, imperatives, behaviour, patterns, attitudes' rather than recognizing that values are tied to the world.

anti_patterns.description: Allowing personal prejudices and ego to cloud perception.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It prevents the subject from seeing things as they really are, which is necessary for both true aesthetic appreciation and ethical behavior.
